text: Atomistic simulations are indispensable tools for fields ranging from drug discovery to materials science. Recently, machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have emerged as powerful alternatives to costly ab initio calculations. A transformative advancement for the field would be the development of a "universal" reactive MLIP—one capable of accurately modeling diverse chemical transformations across various environments without requiring system-specific retraining. In this work, we introduce ANI-1xnr, a general-purpose reactive MLIP developed through the automated sampling of chemical reactions in condensed phases. To demonstrate its versatility, we applied ANI-1xnr to five complex and distinct chemical processes: the nucleation of solid-phase carbon, the formation of graphene rings from acetylene, the degradation of biofuel additives, methane combustion, and the prebiotic synthesis of glycine from small molecules. In each case, ANI-1xnr produced results in close agreement with experimental data and established theoretical benchmarks. Our findings suggest that ANI-1xnr is a robust and highly transferable model for C, H, N, and O chemistry, providing a scalable foundation for high-throughput in silico exploration of reactive systems.
